coredata.dc:title: Beyond carbon dots: Intrinsic reducibility in Ti3C2 MXene quantum dots induces ultrasensitive fluorescence detection and scavenging of Mn(VII)
coredata.dc:creator: Gu, Jiangjiang
Lu, Xingchang
Li, Guangjing
Shan, Baoliang
Liu, Jiahao
Qu, Yaxin
Ye, Huan
Xi, Kai
Wu, Honghong
coredata.dc:description: Recently, cutting two-dimension MXenes into quantum dots (QDs) becomes a hotspot due to the introduction of photoluminescence properties with concomitantly extensive applications. However, it is still a challenge to utilize MXene QDs (MQDs) for the simultaneous detection and scavenging heavy metal ions that may threaten environment safety and human health. Herein, MQDs are prepared by hydrothermal treatment of Ti3C2 MXene in the presence of ethylenediamine and oxidized by H2O2 to obtain carbon dots (CDs). These MQDs and CDs with various functional groups show excellent dispersibility, small size (less than 10 nm), good fluorescence, and high stability. Then, Ti3C2 MQDs are firstly utilized to establish the fluorescent probe towards Mn(VII) with limit of detection (LOD) as low as 5.2 nM. The ultrasensitive selectivity of Ti3C2 MQDs is attributed to their inherent reducibility, which can cause the redox reaction between Ti3C2 MQDs and Mn(VII) with precipitous fluorescence quenching and elimination of Mn(VII). After the lack of reducibility, CDs show selective response to Mn(VII) with LOD of 230 nM based on synergistic inner-filter effect (IFE) and static quenching. Furthermore, the MQDs are successfully applied as a dual functional platform to evaluate the level of Mn (VII) and scavenge it in plant leaves. The comparation of MQDs and CDs for Mn(VII) detection and scavenging will bring more inspiration about intrinsic properties of QDs derived from MXenes for their composite material design and multifunctional use.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the synthesis procedure to obtain MQDs and CDs derived from Ti3AlC2 MAX powders though etching and hydrothermal treatment approaches; SEM images of Ti3AlC2 MAX bulk (b) and multilayer Ti3C2 MXene (c); TEM images and the responding size distribution histograms of MQDs (d) and CDs (e), inset: HRTEM images of an individual nanoparticle with lattice fringes.

(a) XRD patterns of Ti3AlC2 MAX, Ti3C2 MXene, MQDs, CDs and the standard card (JCPDS No. 52–0875); (b) XPS full survey of MQDs and CDs; high-resolution C 1 s XPS spectra of MQDs (c) and CDs (d) (black solid: raw, colored solid: fitting, black dash: background); UV–vis absorption spectrum (Abs.), optimal PL excitation and emission spectra (PLE&PL) of MQDs (e) and CDs (f), inset: photograph of aqueous solutions under visible light and 365 nm UV.

(a) The selectivity of MQDs and CDs for the fluorescence detection of Mn(VII): normalized PL intensity histograms of MQDs (0.1 mg/mL) and CDs (0.1 mg/mL) in presence of various ions (20 μM for MQDs, 200 μM for CDs); (b) scatter plots between ΔI/I0 of MQDs and Mn(VII) concentration in the range of 0–20 μM (ΔI = I0-I), inset: linear relationship/correlation between ΔI/I0 of MQDs and Mn(VII) concentration in the range of 0–1.7 μM; (c) linear scatter plots between I0/I of CDs and Mn(VII) concentration in the range of 0–200 μM; I0 represented the PL intensity of MQDs or CDs in absence of Mn(VII), and I represented the PL intensity of MQDs or CDs in addition of various ions (a) or different concentrations of Mn(VII) (b, c). Error bars were standard deviations from triplicate detection.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra and photographs of Mn(VII) solution and MQDs+Mn(VII); (b) the high-resolution Mn XPS spectrum of MQDs+Mn(VII); (c) time-resolved fluorescence decay curves and fluorescence lifetime of MQDs, MQDs+Mn(VII), CDs, and CDs+Mn(VII); (d) the PL emission spectra of MQDs, MQDs+Mn(VII) and MQDs+Mn(VII)+AA under 320 nm excitation; (e) the PL emission spectra of CDs, CDs+Mn(VII) and CDs+Mn(VII)+AA under 350 nm excitation; (f) schematic description for the detection mechanism based on fluorescent MQDs and CDs towards Mn(VII).

Observation and scavenging of Mn(VII) in plant leaves based on MQDs. (a) Arabidopsis thaliana leaves treated with TES (control), MQDs, and MQDs+Mn(VII) (red circles: injection sites); (b) confocal fluorescence imaging of blank, MQDs, MQDs+Mn(VII) in arabidopsis thaliana leaves under dark field, chloroplasts and overlay; (c) the statistical fluorescence intensities of dark filed in Fig. 5b. The Scale bar in Fig. 5b was 20 μm.
Analytical results of MQDs for Mn(VII) detection in environmental water samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Beyond carbon dots: Intrinsic reducibility in Ti3C2 MXene quantum dots induces ultrasensitive fluorescence detection and scavenging of Mn(VII)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, cutting two-dimension MXenes into quantum dots (QDs) becomes a hotspot due to the introduction of photoluminescence properties with concomitantly extensive applications. However, it is still a challenge to utilize MXene QDs (MQDs) for the simultaneous detection and scavenging heavy metal ions that may threaten environment safety and human health. Herein, MQDs are prepared by hydrothermal treatment of Ti3C2 MXene in the presence of ethylenediamine and oxidized by H2O2 to obtain carbon dots (CDs). These MQDs and CDs with various functional groups show excellent dispersibility, small size (less than 10 nm), good fluorescence, and high stability. Then, Ti3C2 MQDs are firstly utilized to establish the fluorescent probe towards Mn(VII) with limit of detection (LOD) as low as 5.2 nM. The ultrasensitive selectivity of Ti3C2 MQDs is attributed to their inherent reducibility, which can cause the redox reaction between Ti3C2 MQDs and Mn(VII) with precipitous fluorescence quenching and elimination of Mn(VII). After the lack of reducibility, CDs show selective response to Mn(VII) with LOD of 230 nM based on synergistic inner-filter effect (IFE) and static quenching. Furthermore, the MQDs are successfully applied as a dual functional platform to evaluate the level of Mn (VII) and scavenge it in plant leaves. The comparation of MQDs and CDs for Mn(VII) detection and scavenging will bring more inspiration about intrinsic properties of QDs derived from MXenes for their composite material design and multifunctional use.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the youngest members in two-dimension (2D) layered nanomaterials, early transition metal carbides/nitrides and carbonitrides (MXenes) have aroused enormous interests since they were discovered in 2011 [1–3]. MXenes are made up of a general formula of Mn+1XnTx, where M refers to the early transition metal (e.g. Ti, Mo, Nb and V), X represents for C or/and N element, n = 1 to 3, while Tx stands the hydrophilic surface terminating groups, including –OH, -O-, and -F [4,5]. Based on the properties of high surface area with abundant active sites, metallic conductivity, strong absorption and superior hydrophilicity, MXenes exhibit great promise for applications in energy storage/convention, electronic devices, catalysis, membrane separation, antibacterial agents and recent biomedicine/sensing [6–13]. However, the lack of photoluminescence (PL) property in MXenes restricts their use in optical and bio-labeling fields.

Top-down cutting of 2D materials into quantum dots (QDs) with common size less than 10 nm is an efficient approach to endow layer materials with PL properties, which is usually owing to the quantum confinement and surface defects [14,15]. This strategy has been successfully applied to graphene, hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN), graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), black phosphorus, and transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) [16–20], which is also predicted to be useful on MXenes and finally proved experimentally in 2017 [21]. Hydrothermal treatment and ultrasound are usually utilized to obtain MXene QDs (MQDs), which demonstrate excitation-dependent emission fluorescence, excellent water solubility and good biocompatibility [22–24]. Although various MQDs are used as bio-imaging markers, chemical or biological sensors, photothermal therapy agents, immune adjuvants, energy storage materials, catalysts/cocatalysts, and photoelectric devices [25–33], it seems that MQDs have no unique features compared with reported QDs derived from other 2D materials and emerging carbon dots (CDs). Moreover, the special properties in MQDs derived from MXenes have been rarely investigated.

Reducibility is regarded as one of the most important properties in MXenes, which is mainly resulted from the impending bonds and active sites of Ti and other early transition metal element atoms [34,35]. The reducibility of MXenes is adequately utilized to construct composite materials (e.g. 2D TiO2, Au or Ag nanoparticles bonded on Ti3C2 MXene) though redox reaction with certain oxidants [36–38]. Recently, some MQDs are confirmed to possess intrinsic reducibility derived from the MXene precursors, which is helpful for realizing their specific new usage. For example, the reducibility in MQDs was employed to construct fluorescent probes that had selective response to Fe(III) though partial reduction [39,40]. MQDs with reducibility can also be used as antioxidative agents to scavenge free radicals with strong oxidability such as reactive oxygen species (ROS) [41–43]. In fact, weather the intrinsic reducibility in MQDs can induce distinctive property beyond other QDs is still unknown.

In this report, to show reducibility of MQDs can offer advantages beyond other QDs, Ti3C2 MQDs and CDs are prepared and their fluorescence detection performance and mechanism towards Mn(VII) are compared. The Ti3C2 MQDs with reducibility are synthesized through a facile hydrothermal method, which can form CDs via further oxidation. Both of the obtained MQDs and CDs exhibit excitation-dependent photoluminescence property with excellent aqueous solubility. Importantly, the MQDs can act as a “turn-off” probe for the fluorescent determination of Mn(VII) with high sensitivity (LOD = 5.2 nM) in the linear range of 0–1.7 μM, which is the first reported ultrasensitive fluorescent probe towards Mn(VII) based on QDs. The probable reason is that the intrinsic reducibility in MQDs induce the redox reaction between MQDs and Mn(VII), resulting in scavenging of Mn(VII). While the reducibility are removed, the obtained CDs can be selectively quenched by Mn(VII) with the LOD of 230 nM (linear range: 0–200 μM) though inner-filter effect (IFE) and static quenching. This detection platform of MQDs is successfully applied for the efficient evaluation of Mn(VII) concentration in actual environmental water samples and in vivo for plant leaves for the first time, which has drawn people’s attention to the unique features in MQDs derived from MXene and the induced special applications.

Ti3AlC2 (99.9%) was bought from Forsman Scientific Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Hydrofluoric acid (HF, 40%), ethylenediamine (EDA), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%), KMnO4, HCl, KCl, NaCl, AgNO3, MgSO4, CaCl2, MnCl2, CdCl2, FeCl2, CuCl2, NiCl2, PbCl2, CH3COOZn, HgNO2, CoCl3, FeCl3, AlCl3, CrCl3, NaF, KCl, KBr, KI, KNO3, NaNO2, CH3COONa, NaH2PO4, Na2S, Na2SO4, Na2S2O8, K2FeO4, K2Cr2O7, Na2HPO4, Na2CO3 and Na3PO4 were bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Quinine sulfate was obtained from Adamas-beta Inc. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals without labeled concentration were of analytical grade and used as received. Ultrapure water (18.25 MΩ cm−1) provided by the Millipore system was utilized throughout this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and purification of multilayer Ti3C2 MXene
ce:section.ce:para: Ti3AlC2 (1 g) powder was gradually immersed in 20 mL HF acid (40%) and stirred for 24 h at room temperature (25 °C) to remove the Al layer as reported before [44]. Then the suspension solution was centrifuged and rinsed multiple times with ultrapure water until pH approached 7 to discard the supernatant HF and impurities. The final product (Ti3C2 MXene pellet) was dried at 60 °C under vacuum for 12 h and stored in −20 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and purification of MQDs and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: MQDs were synthesized by the one-pot hydrothermal method. In brief, 1 mL of EDA was added into 200 mL (5 mg/mL) of multilayer Ti3C2 MXene and the mixture was treated with ultrasonication bath (1000 W) for several minutes. Then, the suspension solution was transferred into a 250 mL micro high pressure reactor at 120 °C for 8 h in an N2 protected environment. After cooling at room temperature, the resulting black suspension was centrifuged and filtered through a 220 nm microporous membrane to remove unreacted MXene. The resulted solution pH was adjusted with HCl equal 7 followed by concentrating using rotary evaporator. Finally, the solution was subjected to dialysis for 2 days (MWCO = 500) and the MQDs could be obtained by freeze-drying.

CDs were prepared by two-pot hydrothermal process by adding H2O2 in MQDs. In brief, the first step referred to the preparation process of MQDs and the solution containing MQDs was obtained after the hydrothermal process and centrifugation. Then 1 mL of 30% H2O2 was added into MQDs and the mixer was further heated in the same micro high pressure reactor under the condition of 120 °C for 8 h reaction. The product of CDs was obtained though the same treatment process as MQDs including cooling, suspension, filtration, pH adjustion, dialysis and freeze-drying.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were carried by a SU8020 electron microscope (Hitachi, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were observed by an H-7650 electron microscope (Hitachi, Japan) at an 80 kV accelerating voltage. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were captured by a Talos F200C electron microscope (FEI, USA) with a 200 kV accelerating voltage. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were obtained on a Dimension Icon microscope (Bruker, Germany) in the tapping mode. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and Zeta potential results were conducted using a Nano-ZS90 Zetasizer laser particle sizer (Malvern, UK). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were carried out by using a D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy were performed on a NEXUS 670 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA) in the range from 400 to 4000 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) results were obtained using a K-Alpha XPS instrument (Thermo Scientific, USA). Raman spectrum was measured on a LabRAM HR Evolution spectrometer (Horiba Scientific, Japan). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu 2450 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). All fluorescence emission and excitation spectra were recorded on a RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Time-resolved fluorescence decay was conducted on a TimePro-01 lifetime fluorescence spectrometer (HORIBA, France). Fluorescence images were captured by using a Leica SP8 confocal microscope (Leica Microsystems, Germany).

In this equation, “s” and “r” referred to the sample and reference, respectively. The quinine sulfate was dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 as reference and its QY was known as 0.54 at 340 nm excitation. “I” represented the integrated emission intensity of fluorescent spectra at 340 nm excitation. “A” was the UV–vis absorbance value at 340 nm, controlled between 0.1 and 0.01 to avoid re-absorption. “η” was the refractive index for the solvent.

The fluorescent decay curves could be fitted by a double-exponential function [46]

In this equation, A1 and A2 were the corresponding amplitudes, τ1 and τ2 were the time constants of the two radiative decay channels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and quantitative measurements for Mn(VII)
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of Mn(VII) was performed at room temperature with pH = 7.0. In a typical assay, 20 μL of MQDs aqueous solution (pH = 7.0) was dispersed into ultrapure water (pH = 7.0), then 20 μL of Mn(VII) solution (pH = 7.0) was added, and the final volume of Mn(VII) was 4 mL with the concentration range of 0–20 μM. For comparison, the same procedure was performed for other ions with the final concentration of 20 μM. In addition, 50 μL of CDs aqueous solution was dispersed into ultrapure water (pH = 7.0), then 50 μL of Mn(VII) solution was added, and the final volume of Mn(VII) was 4 mL with the concentration range of 0–200 μM. For comparison, the same procedure was performed for other ions with the final concentration of 200 μM. The emission spectra of MQDs and CDs were recorded at 320 and 350 nm excitation, respectively.

MCF-7 cells (105 cells/mL) were cultured in a 96-well microplate (100 μL per well) for 12 h at 37 °C with 5% CO2. Then the MQDs+Mn(VII) was added into the well to make the final concentrations of MQDs and Mn(VII) were 0.1 mg/mL and 1 μM, respectively. After incubating for 24 h, the MCF-7 cells were washed with PBS (pH = 7.4) and added with 20 μL MTT solution (5 mg/mL) and 100 μL DMEM for 4 h. Then 150 μL DMSO was introduced after removing the culture medium. The obtained mixture was shaken for 10 min, and the optical density (OD) at 490 nm was measured by an En-Spire multimode plate reader. The control group was added with PBS to the cells. The cell viability was calculated according to the equation as below:
(3)
Cell viability
= 100 % ×
OD sam
/
OD con
In this equation, ODsam and ODcon were the OD values of the sample and control groups, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of Mn(II) content in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The actual environmental water samples were collected from tap water, the East lake (Wuhan, China), the Yangtze river (Wuhan, China). Then these obtained samples were filtered with 0.22 μM membranes to remove the large particles, and the pH was regulated to 7.0. All the fluorescence emission spectra of solutions were recorded under 320 nm excitation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of Mn(VII) content in plant leaves
ce:section.ce:para: Arabidopsis thaliana plants (Col-0) at the 4 weeks stage were grown in growth room with the settings: 70% relative humidity, 200 μmol m−2 s−1 photosynthetic active radiation (PAR), 25±2 °C (growth temperature), and 14/10 h as the day/night regime.

MQDs were with diluted with TES {2-[(2-Hydroxy-1,1-bis(hydroxymethyl)ethyl)amino] ethanesulfonic acid with 10 mM at pH = 7} and the final concentration was 0.5 mg/mL. Plant leaves were injected with 100 μL TES (10 mM), MQDs (0.5 mg/mL) and MQDs (0.5 mg/mL) for control, MQDs and MQDs+Mn(VII) groups, respectively. After incubation in the dark for 2 h, the leaves of MQDs+Mn(VII) group were injected with 50 μL Mn(VII) solutions (100 nM, TES, pH = 7) for 1 h in darkness. Then the fluorescence of injected leaves was observed by a Leica confocal microscope. The confocal imaging settings were set as below: 40 × wet objective, 405 nm laser excitation, PMT1: 450–500 nm, PMT2: 700–790 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of MQDs and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation methods of Ti3C2 MXene, MQDs and CDs are demonstrated in Fig. 1 a. Ti3C2 MXene was prepared by etching Ti3AlC2 MAX phase, and functional groups such Ti-F, Ti-OH and Ti-O-Ti were modified on Ti3C2 MXene in this etching process and layered structure was obtained [47,48]. Then MQDs were obtained through a facile hydrothermal cutting treatment of the layered Ti3C2 MXene in the presence of ethylenediamine (EDA). Furthermore, MQDs could be treated with hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) via another hydrothermal process, and CDs could be finally synthesized.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) were firstly used to study the morphology of Ti3AlC2 MAX and the as-prepared Ti3C2 MXene. As demonstrated in Fig. 1b, Ti3AlC2 MAX were irregular bulk with several micron size. After the etching process, Ti3C2 MXene showed muti-layered structure like accordion with about 5 μm (Fig. 1c). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was further used to investigate the morphology of the obtained MQDs and CDs. In Fig. 1d and e, both MQDs and CDs were well dispersible small nanoparticles, with statistical size (by counting 100 particles) of 3.62 nm (Fig. 1d inset) and 3.05 nm (Fig. 1e inset) for MQDs and CDs, respectively. The atomic force microscopy (AFM) results were displayed in Fig. S1, MQDs had the thickness less than 2 nm and CDs had the thickness less than 5 nm. The difference of MQDs and CDs in AFM images was contributed to that MQDs might contain a few layers derived from the Ti3C2 MXene, and CDs displayed quasi-spherical morphology formed in the hydrothermal carbonization and polymerization process [49]. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) results (Fig. S2) revealed that MQDs had an average size around 3.8 nm and it was 3.4 nm for CDs, which basically matched the results of TEM and AFM. The typical high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images in Fig. 1d and e inset were next used to determine the core structure of the MQDs and CDs. MQDs had a lattice spacing of 0.266 nm, which was ascribed to the (0110) crystal face of Ti3C2 MXene and verified the MXene structure in MQDs [50,51]. While CDs possessed the lattice spacing of 0.212 nm, corresponding to the (110) graphite carbon face [52]. The difference in HRTEM images implied the structure difference of MQDs and CDs.

To investigate the structure of obtained Ti3C2 MXene, MQDs and CDs, X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured. As showed in Fig. 2 a, it revealed Ti3AlC2 MAX corresponded well with the standard PDF card (No. 52–0875). After the etching process, disappearance of the peak (2θ = 39°) implied that Al was completely removed by the etching process. The shift of peak at 10° (002) revealed the enlarge of interlayer spacing, and other diffraction peaks were consistent with the previous reports, indicating the successful preparation of Ti3C2 MXene [53]. Compared with Ti3C2 MXene, the (002) diffraction peak of MQDs shifted from 8.9° to 6.4°, indicating the enlarge distance [54]. The peak at 2θ = 60° was still maintained, indicating the structure of Ti3C2 MXene kept after the hydrothermal cutting process [55]. While further hydrothermal treatment was carried on and the CDs were obtained, their XRD pattern only had a broad peak at about 23.8° and the characteristic peaks of Ti3C2 MXene disappeared, which indicated the Ti3C2 MXene structure had been destroyed totally [21]. The Zeta potential values of different samples were displayed in Fig. S3, −35.1 ± 2.5 mV for Ti3C2 MXene, 4.0±1.0 mV for MQDs and −2.3 ± 0.9 mV for CDs. The transformation of charge from Ti3C2 MXene to MQDs was might distributed to the modification of EDA on MQDs, then further hydrothermal treatment might result in more carboxyl groups in CDs and made the charge negative again.

To further verify the functional groups of MQDs and CDs, Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was used. As shown in Fig. S4, Ti3C2 MXene did not have obvious peaks. MQDs and CDs had several same identical stretching vibrations, such as –OH at about 3430 cm−1, O-H at 1390 cm−1, C=O at 1650 cm−1, C-F at around 1100 cm−1, and Ti-F around 809 cm−1 [56]. In addition, the peak at 665 cm−1 might account for the vibration of the Ti-O bond [57]. The vibration at 3120 and 1561 cm−1 were attributed to –NH groups in MQDs and CDs, indicating that both of the MQDs and CDs were doped by nitrogen. And C=O had been broad in CDs, which was maybe caused by the more carboxyl groups. Both MQDs and CDs had lots of hydrophilic functional groups, resulting in the excellent dispersibility in water. Furthermore, the surface composition MQDs and CDs were studied using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). In Fig. 2b, both MQDs and CDs had five peaks at 284.0, 399.0, 455.2, 531.3 and 584.7 eV, attributing to C 1 s, N 1 s, Ti 2p, O 1 s and F 1 s, respectively. The elemental analysis results in Table S1 indicated that the composition of C: N: Ti: O: F were 57.73%: 8.82%: 10.64%: 22.74%: 6.07% and 35.90%: 7.60%: 1.07%: 49.34%: 6.09% for MQDs and CDs, respectively. The result indicated that the successful doping of N element in the prepared quantum dots and most of Ti in MQDs was etched away during the hydrothermal and oxidizing process. The obvious decrease of Ti content from MQDs to CDs was caused by the destroy of Ti3C2 MXene structure, and the O content of CDs increased obviously due to the hydrothermal oxidation. The C 1 s spectrum of MQDs (Fig. 2c) revealed the presence of C-Ti (281.3 eV), C-C (284.8 eV), C-N (286.0 eV) and C-F/C=O (288.1 eV) functional groups, while C 1 s spectrum of CDs (Fig. 2d) only had C-C/C=C (284.8 eV), C-O/C-N (286.4 eV) and C-F/C=O (288.4 eV) peaks. The absence of Ti-C in the XPS C 1 s spectrum of CDs was ascribed to the destroy of Ti3C2 MXene structure in the formation of CDs, which was consistent with the XRD results. Other element spectra were displayed in Fig. S5. For Ti 2p spectrum of MQDs (Fig. S5a), Ti-C 2p3/2 (454.8 eV), Ti-F 2p3/2 (456.2 eV) and Ti-O 2p3/2 (458.4 eV) bonds were shown, while only Ti-O 2p3/2 (458.6 eV) existed in Ti 2p spectrum of CDs (Fig. S5b) [39]. The residual Ti doped in the CDs was in the form of Ti-O due to the complete oxidation of Ti-C in the Ti3C2 MXene structure [21]. N 1 s of MQDs (Fig. S5c) contained N-C (399.6 eV) and N-H (401.5 eV) peaks, and N1s of CDs (Fig. S5d) were to N-C (399.9 eV), N-H (401.5 eV), and NO3 (406, 7 eV) [21]. For O 1 s, MQDs (Fig. S5e) had O-Ti (529.6 eV) and Ti-OH/O=C (531.7 eV), and CDs (Fig. S5f) had O=C (531.8 eV), C-OH/C-O-C (532.2 eV) and O=N (540.9 eV). The F 1 s of MQDs (Fig. S5g) had the structure of F-C (684.6 eV) and F-Ti (685.9 eV), while CDs (Fig. S5h) only had F-C at 684.9 eV. The XPS results gave an accurate description of the exact structure of prepared MQDs and CDs, which were consistent with the FTIR spectra. To confirm the successful preparation of CDs, Raman spectrum was measured and the result was shown in Fig. S6. It exhibited two peaks at 1381 and 1576 cm−1, which were corresponding to the D and G bands in CDs [58].

UV–vis absorption and PL emission spectra were explored to show the optical properties of MQDs and CDs. As displayed in Fig. 2e, MQDs had two absorption peaks at about 227 and 344 nm, which were inferred to be caused by the π→π* and n→π* transitions [59]. The inset photographs in Fig. 2e displayed MQDs under 365 nm irradiation showed a bright blue-green fluorescence. Then, the PL 2D emission spectra of MQDs were measured at different excitation wavelengths and shown in Fig. S7a. MQDs showed an excitation-dependent feature that could be attributed to the core and defects of MQDs [60]. The optimal emission and excitation wavelengths were located at 473 nm and 320 nm (Fig. 2e). In addition, the PL excitation (PLE) spectrum showed two peaks, which was similar to some reported MQDs [21,54]. As shown in Fig. 2f, CDs showed two absorption peaks at about 265 and 321 nm, which were resulted from π→π* and n→π* transitions, respectively [61]. The inset photographs in Fig. 2f displayed CDs under 365 nm irradiation showed a bright blue fluorescence, which was different from that of MQDs. The PL 2D emission spectra of CDs were shown in Fig. S7b, CDs also showed an excitation-dependent feature and the optimal emission and excitation wavelengths were at 442 nm and 350 nm (Fig. 2f). The peak wavelength in PLE spectrum of CDs was larger than that of the UV absorption peak, which was reported in previous CDs [45,46,48]. The quantum yield (QY) values of MQDs and CDs were calculated as 6.6% and 2.7% (Table S2) by using quinine sulfate as the reference.

The pH, temperature, ionic strength, and Xenon lamp irradiation time effects on the fluorescence of as-fabricated MQDs and CDs were examined in detail. Fig. S8a exhibited the pH effect on the fluorescence intensity of MQDs and CDs. At pH of 1–13, the increase of pH made the fluorescence increased at first and kept constant and then decreased. The MQDs showed a high PL intensity from pH = 3 to 11 and CDs performed a high fluorescence intensity from pH = 3 to 10. The strong acid and alkaline environment could cause the PL intensity reduction of MQDs and CDs, possibly due to the change of functional groups during the protonation and deprotonation process [22,40,58,59]. The temperature effect on MQDs and CDs was also investigated. As shown in Fig. S8b. The fluorescence intensity of MQDs were still stable in the range of 0–40 °C. With different concentrations of NaCl (Fig. S8c), the PL intensity of MQDs and CDs did not show obvious change, which was conducive for practical applications. Fig. S8d showed no distinct photobleaching (>90%) after the Xenon lamp irradiation of MQDs and CDs for 180 min, suggesting that both of MQDs and CDs had good photostability. Furthermore, to investigate the stability of MQDs and CDs under strong acid and alkali environments, the time-dependent PL intensity of MQDs and CDs under pH = 1 and 13 was tested. As shown in Fig. S9, the PL intensity of MQDs decreased with the increase of time, suggesting that they could decompose possibly due to the accelerated oxidation of the MQDs in strong acid and alkali solutions [40]. And the PL intensity of CDs was stable under the same conditions, indicating that they could keep the structure with different pH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent detection performance and mechanism of MQDs and CDs to Mn(VII)
ce:section.ce:para: Mn(VII) is a hazardous heavy metal ion with strong oxidizing ability, which has received extensive attentions because it exists widely in medicinal herb and water samples [62,63]. To survey the selectivity and interference of MQDs in Mn(VII) detection, different ions were introduced into the MQDs aqueous solution, including thirty-four kinds of common cations (K+, Na+, Ag+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Co2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Cr3+), and anions (F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, NO3 –, NO2 –, CH3COO–, H2PO4 −, S2−, SO4 2−, S2SO4 2−, FeO4 2−, Cr2O7 2−, HPO4 2−, CO3 2–, PO4 3− and MnO4−). As shown in Fig. S10a, Mn(VII) afforded the most obvious fluorescence quenching phenomenon which was shown clearly in Fig. 3 a. Other ions could not cause significant intensity change, indicating MQDs could realize the highly selective sensing of Mn(VII). Especially, the addition of Fe(III) into MQDs didn't cause significant intensity change, possibly because that the MQDs only processed a weak oxidizing ability which could not induce the reduction of Fe(III). Moreover, the possible interference ions were evaluated to investigate the selectivity of this sensing system (Fig. S11a). It was found that most of metal ions did not induce obvious quenching on PL intensity of MQDs except for Mn(VII), demonstrating a good antijamming capability for the approved method to determinate Mn(VII). Therefore, the MQDs-based “turn-off” fluorescent probe possessed superiorities of sensitivity and simplicity for the label-free determination of Mn(VII). The CDs also had selectivity for Mn(VII) detection. In Fig. S10b, different ions were added into CDs solutions and Mn(VII) caused the highest quenching. Other ions did not inter much except for Cr(VI), which could cause part quenching of CDs in Fig. 3a. The possible interference was also added into CDs to detect Mn(VII) in Fig. S11b and revealed the good quenching of CDs was realized in presence of Mn(VII).

The pH effect on MQDs and CDs for the Mn(VII) detection were studied in Fig. S12, which indicated that Mn(VII) could quenched the fluorescence of MQDs and CDs in a wide pH range. The quenching efficiency values of MQDs and CDs for Mn(VII) were stable between pH of 3–11, and the working solution pH was set as 7. The time-dependent PL intensity of MQDs and CDs with Mn(VII) were also tested in Fig. S13. It indicated that the PL intensity decreased quickly after the addition of Mn(VII) into MQDs and CDs and was stabilized within 2 min and 1 min for MQDs and CDs, respectively. Thus, the incubation time for Mn(VII) detection was set as 2 min, which showed a fast response. The concentration of MQDs or CDs was 0.1 mg/mL because of the appropriate fluorescence intensity on the fluorescence spectrometer.

As shown in Fig. S14a, an obvious fluorescence quenching could be observed by the addition of Mn(VII) ranging from 0 to 20 μM. Fig. 3b further represented the relationship between ΔI/I0 (I0 and I were the PL intensities of MQDs in absence and presence of Mn(VII)) and different concentrations of Mn(VII), showing an excellent linear curve in the range from 0 to 1.7 μM. And the detection limit was calculated as 5.2 nM (S/N = 3), which was the lowest limit of detection (LOD) for Mn(VII) detection (Table S3). For CDs (Fig. S14b), the Mn(VII) was added in ranging from 0 to 200 μM. Fig. 3c further represented the relation between I0/I (I0 and I represented the PL intensities of CDs in absence and presence of Mn(VII)) and Mn(VII) concentration, also with an linear behavior in the range of 0–200 μM. Then, the Stern–Volmer equation was obtained to be I0/I = 0.998 + KSV[Mn(VII)], where KSV referred to the SV quenching constant. The KSV was estimated to be 0.598 with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.994. And the LOD was calculated as 230 nM (S/N = 3). Our results implied that the proposed fluorescence probe based on MQDs and CDs were suitable to determinate the Mn(VII) concentration in pure water.

To explain the mechanism of fluorescence quenching for MQDs and CDs for Mn(VII) detection, UV–vis absorption spectra of Mn(VII) solution and MQDs+Mn(VII) were measured. As shown in Fig. 4 a, Mn(VII) aqueous solution had a characteristic absorption peak at about 525 nm, which decreased dramatically after adding MQDs. And the light pink color of Mn(VII) was faded, showing that MQDs had scavenging activity for Mn(VII). To obtain the information of reaction product, XPS was tested after the addition of Mn(VII) into MQDs. The full survey was showed in Fig. S15a, an obvious Mn 2p could be observed. As depicted in Fig. 4b, the Mn 2p3/2 XPS spectrum could be fitted to 641.0, 642.2, 643.4 and 646.1 eV, which were due to the presence of Mn(II), Mn(III) and Mn(IV) and might be attributed to the Mn3O4 [64]. To demonstrate the environmental friendliness of the reduction product of Mn(VII), the cytotoxicity test was conducted with MCF-7 cells by the MTT assay. Fig. S16 showed that MQDs+Mn(VII) did not result in significant toxicity after 24 h incubation. The low valence states of Mn element confirmed the reduction ability of MQDs for scavenging of Mn(VII). The Ti 2p spectrum of MQDs+Mn(VII) (Fig. S15b) showed that all Ti was present as Ti-O, indicating that Ti-C in MQDs was totally destroyed and oxidized by Mn(VII). It could be inferred that Ti-C bonds in MQDs were the specified interaction sites for Mn(VII) [42,43]. Moreover, we added the AgNO3 into MQDs solution and could observe the synthesis of Ag nanoparticles with size about 60 nm (Fig. S17), and the inset image indicated the (200) of AgNPs with space of 0.204 nm, confirming the reductivity of MQDs [65]. In addition, to understand the fluorescence quenching mechanism between MQDs fluorophore and Mn(VII) absorber, the lifetimes of MQDs, MQDs+Mn(VII), CDs, and CDs+Mn(VII) were measured. The fluorescence decay results were showed in Fig. 4c, and the relevant lifetime calculation data were shown in Table S4. According to the best fitting data, the average lifetimes of MQDs and MQDs+Mn(VII) were 7.40 and 5.40 ns, respectively, indicating that there was an fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process between MQDs and the target Mn(VII) [66]. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching of MQDs by adding Mn(VII) was attributed to the dynamic quenching. While for CDs, the average lifetimes of CDs and CDs+Mn(VII) are 5.81 and 5.27 ns, which meant the no obvious change of fluorescent lifetime and that static quenching occurred when CDs met Mn(VII) [67]. To understand the mechanism of CDs for Mn(VII) detection, the UV–vis adsorption spectrum of Mn(VII) displayed multiple band peaks in Fig. S18, which demonstrated a good overlap with the maximum fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the prepared CDs and enabled the inner-filter effect (IFE) based quenching [68]. Thus, the synergistic IFE and static quenching could contribute to the Mn(VII) detection based on CDs [69].

For further understanding the mechanism, ascorbic acid (AA) was used to test the fluorescence recovery of MQDs+Mn(VII) and CDs+Mn(VII). AA is a frequently used reductant that can induce the redox reaction with some high-valence-state heavy metal ions, such as Cr(VI) and Mn(VII) [70]. As shown in this study, MQDs could reduce Mn(VII) and their structure were destroyed in this redox process. If AA was added into MQDs+Mn(VII), the quenched fluorescence could not be restored. On the other side, CDs could detect Mn(VII) though fluorescence quenching due to the synergistic inner-filter effect (IFE) and static quenching mechanism. The IFE was caused by the good overlap between the adsorption peaks in UV spectrum of Mn(VII) and the maximum fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs. When AA was added into CDs+Mn(VII), the Mn(VII) could be reduced and the absence of Mn(VII) would remove the IFE between CDs and Mn(VII). After the addition of AA, MQDs+Mn(VII) could not restore the fluorescence, indicating the destroy of MQDs after adding Mn(VII) (Fig. 4d). By added the AA, CDs+Mn(VII) could restore the fluorescence to almost the initial level and proved that the Mn(VII) was not reduced and the IFE effect could work (Fig. 4e). The mechanism could be summarized as in Fig. 4f, when Mn(VII) was introduced to MQDs, the redox reaction happened and fluorescence was quenched. When Mn(VII) was added into CDs, the IFE and static quenching would result in the quenching of fluorescence. And the ultra-sensitive performance of MQDs could be ascribed to the redox reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of Mn(VII) level in actual water and plant samples
ce:section.ce:para: For the real sample testing, the tap water, East Laker, and Yangtze River water were directly collected for utilization and measurement. The analytical results were demonstrated in Table 1 . When different concentrations of Mn(VII) were added into the environmental water samples with MQDs, the detected recovery efficiencies were in the range from 101.0 to 105.0% and the corresponding relative standard deviation (RSD) was of 1.32–3.51%. These results revealed that MQDs could be applied for the determination of Mn(VII) level in some practical samples with satisfaction. To further confirm the feasibility of this fluorescent probe, MQDs for Mn(VII) detection in the real tap water samples with different pH was tested and showed in Table S5. It demonstrated that MQDs showed fine performance (recovery: 100.5–108.4%, RSD: 2.33–3.69%) for Mn(VII) detection in real water samples in a wide range of pH (5–11). The acidic environment (pH = 3) could cause the decrease of found Mn(VII).

The MQDs were also used for observation and scavenging of Mn(VII) in plant leaves. The biocompatibility of MQDs was first tested by using arabidopsis thaliana as a model. As shown in Fig. S19, after injection with different concentrations of MQDs in arabidopsis thaliana leaves for three days, the plants did not show obvious change compared with the control and {2-[(2-Hydroxy-1,1-bis(hydroxymethyl)ethyl)amino] ethanesulfonic acid with 10 mM at pH = 7} (TES) groups, except the minor damage occurred at the injection part when the MODs was above 0.5 mg/mL. Thus, the concentration of MQDs was set as 0.5 mg/mL for the next experiment. As shown in Fig. 5 a, compared with the blank group, the leaves treated with 0.5 mg/mL MQDs for three hours showed a fine status with good biocompatibility. When the leaves were treated with MQDs (0.5 mg/mL) for 2 h, then 50 μL of Mn(VII) (100 nM) was injected for 1 h. The confocal fluorescence images of leaves were shown in Fig. 5b. It was clear that MQDs showed bright fluorescence in leaves, and the fluorescence disappeared after the injection of Mn(VII). The calculated fluorescence intensities of different samples in Fig. 5c demonstrated that the fluorescence intensity increased obviously when the MQDs was injected in the leaves, then it decreased when the Mn(VII) was in present. The results showed the MQDs could be utilized as the efficient fluorescent probes for Mn(VII) detection in vivo for plants.

In summary, MQDs with reducibility were synthesized through a facile hydrothermal treatment of the Ti3C2 MXene precursor, which could furtherly turn into CDs via a hydrothermal oxidation process. These two quantum dots with different fluorescence showed huge difference in the fluorescence “turn-off” detection for Mn(VII). Compared with the CDs for Mn(VII) detection with LOD of 230 nM based on IFE and static quenching, the obtained MQDs had the lowest reported LOD of 5.2 nM, which was at least an order of magnitude lower than the previous work. The ultrasensitive detection of Mn(VII) based on MQDs was attributed to that the intrinsic reducibility in MQDs could cause the reduction of Mn(VII), resulting in the scavenging of Mn(VII). This result verified that the reducibility in MQDs could induce unique advantages than other QDs. Finally, this platform worked both in practical water samples and in plant leaves, which was the first report about MQDs for in vivo imaging and sensing in plants. Although the MQDs can detect and scavenge Mn(VII) in lab and actual environment, the low quantum yield (QY) of MQDs will limit their practical applications and the further work for improving the QY of MQDs is ongoing. Our findings open up a wide range of new potential applications for MQDs and give an inspiration of intrinsic and unique properties of MQDs for more composite nanomaterial construction and usage.
